theme: The Office Knows More Than It Shows

ideas.title: Activity Log Worker Face — A Name Is Not Enough
ideas.description: Every activity log entry renders 'Sofia · QA review #21 complete...' as pure text. The ActivityLogEntry component already has entry.worker (a name string) and there's a working getRoleEmoji(role) function plus the full roster array in App scope. Fix: pass the roster array as a prop to ActivityLog, and in ActivityLogEntry look up the worker by name (roster.find(r => r.name === entry.worker)) to get their emoji. Add a {workerEmoji ?? '🤖'} immediately before the existing activity-icon span. CSS: .activity-worker-emoji { font-size: 0.95rem; width: 22px; flex-shrink: 0; display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; line-height: 1; }. The entry now reads: 🧙 ✅ Sofia · QA review... A face + event icon makes who-did-what parseable at a glance. WHY: The activity log is the most scrolled section of the dashboard. Every entry currently looks identical at arm's length — just color-coded left-border variants. An emoji face anchors the eye before the brain reads the text. Same data, 10x faster to scan. This is the highest-ROI change in the entire codebase right now.

ideas.title: Mobile Dead Zone — Kill the 400px Void Below the Footer
ideas.description: The mobile screenshot (375x812) shows roughly 400px of empty dark background below the footer. The office floor and footer together occupy ~400px of viewport height, leaving the bottom half as a barren void. Root cause: the app shell likely has min-height: 100vh which forces blank space when content is shorter. Targeted fix in App.css: ensure .app-shell uses display: flex; flex-direction: column; min-height: 100dvh (use dvh for mobile browser chrome awareness). Set .app-main { flex: 1; } so it expands to fill available space rather than leaving a gap. If the office-floor container has a hardcoded height on mobile, add: @media (max-width: 600px) { .office-floor-container { height: auto; } html, body { height: 100%; } .app-shell { min-height: 100%; height: 100%; } }. Also verify the tab panel wrappers have no fixed heights that cause overflow. WHY: A 400px black void at the bottom of mobile communicates 'this page didn't finish loading' to every first-time visitor. It's the single most damaging impression on mobile — it undermines every other design decision on the page. One CSS property audit fixes a first impression that affects every user who opens this on their phone.

ideas.title: Error State Demands the Eye — Cards and Desks That Breathe Red
ideas.description: Worker cards with status==='error' get a nameplate color change and a pulsing status dot, but the card body itself has no temporal animation. From 1 meter away, an error card looks almost identical to an idle card — you have to read the badge. Fix in App.css: .worker-card.error { animation: error-card-glow 2s ease-in-out infinite; } @keyframes error-card-glow { 0%,100% { box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.2); border-color: rgba(248,113,113,0.3); } 50% { box-shadow: 0 0 20px 5px rgba(248,113,113,0.35), 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.2); border-color: rgba(248,113,113,0.7); } }. In Office.css: .desk--error { animation: desk-error-glow 2s ease-in-out infinite; } @keyframes desk-error-glow { 0%,100% { box-shadow: none; } 50% { box-shadow: 0 0 10px 3px rgba(248,113,113,0.55); } }. Add prefers-reduced-motion fallback: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .worker-card.error, .desk--error { animation: none; border: 2px solid var(--red); } }. WHY: An error is an action item, not a state. Right now the only signal is a tiny badge that requires reading. A breathing red halo makes the error card find the user's eye — urgency that communicates before comprehension. Sofia can also use this as a QA anchor: if the glow isn't visible for a known error case, the status isn't being set correctly.

ideas.title: Vibe Streak Earns a Crown — Your Score Should Mean Something
ideas.description: vibeStreak is tracked and shown as 'x{n}' when >= 3 via a tiny inline-style span. The streak count is there but the visual payoff is flat. New escalation tiers: at streak >= 3 (steady) add a 👑 crown that pops in beside the vibe pill; at >= 5 (hot) the pill gets a gold shimmer border; at >= 10 (legendary) the pill runs a rainbow hue-rotate. Implementation: wrap the existing vibe pill and streak span in = 10 ? 'legendary' : vibeStreak >= 5 ? 'hot' : vibeStreak >= 3 ? 'steady' : ''}>. Replace the plain x{n} span with: {vibeStreak >= 3 && {vibeStreak >= 10 ? '🏆' : vibeStreak >= 5 ? '🔥' : '👑'}}. CSS: .streak-icon { font-size: 0.7rem; animation: crown-pop 0.4s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both; } @keyframes crown-pop { from{transform:scale(0) rotate(-20deg);opacity:0} to{transform:none;opacity:1} } [data-streak-tier='hot'] .vibe-pill { box-shadow: 0 0 10px rgba(251,191,36,0.45); } [data-streak-tier='legendary'] .vibe-pill { animation: rainbow-shift 2s linear infinite; } @keyframes rainbow-shift { 0%{filter:hue-rotate(0deg)} 100%{filter:hue-rotate(360deg)} }. WHY: A streak mechanic that counts but never escalates is a promise unkept. The 👑 at 3 is instant delight. The 🏆 at 10 turns a passive counter into a goal — people will deliberately keep team vibe high to hit legendary. These are the moments that get screenshotted.

ideas.title: Progress Bar ETA — 'How Long?' Gets an Answer
ideas.description: Worker cards show PROGRESS 34% and 'Working for 18m' but never answer the question every manager has: when will they finish? The data to calculate this already exists: updated_at (when they started working) and progress (how far they are). Simple estimate: ETAmins = round((workingMins / progress) * (100 - progress)). Only show it when progress > 5 (to avoid wild early estimates) and eta < 120 (to avoid nonsense large numbers). In the worker card render in App.jsx, compute: const workingMs = worker.updated_at ? Date.now() - new Date(worker.updated_at).getTime() : null; const workingMins = workingMs ? workingMs / 60000 : 0; const eta = (worker.progress > 5 && workingMins > 1) ? Math.round((workingMins / worker.progress) * (100 - worker.progress)) : null. Render next to the progress percentage label: {eta !== null && eta < 120 && ~{eta}m left}. CSS: .progress-eta { font-size: 0.68rem; color: var(--text-muted); margin-left: auto; font-style: italic; animation: eta-fade 0.4s ease both; } @keyframes eta-fade { from{opacity:0} to{opacity:1} }. WHY: 'PROGRESS 10%' tells you where they are. '~34m left' tells you when the PR will be ready, when the standup can happen, when to stop checking. One calculated number changes how a manager reads the entire dashboard — from wondering to knowing. It also makes the progress bar feel purposeful rather than decorative.

ideas.title: Bloberto Speaks on the Dashboard — The Manager Has No Vacation
ideas.description:
Bloberto fires ambient speech bubbles every 45 seconds on the Office tab, but on the Dashboard tab he's completely invisible — no face, no voice. The most distinctive character in the product disappears the moment you switch views. Fix: extract the per-vibe quote array from Character into a shared utils/quotes.js module exporting getVibeQuotes(vibeKey). Create a tiny hook useBlobertoQuote(vibeKey, intervalMs=45000) that useState-cycles through the array. Add a 'Bloberto's Take' strip to the Dashboard panel, rendered between the stats bar and the workers section:
🫠
“{quote}”
— Bloberto, {teamVibe.label}
. CSS: .bloberto-take { display:flex; align-items:center; gap:0.75rem; padding:0.65rem 1rem; background:rgba(139,92,246,0.07); border-left:3px solid var(--accent); border-radius:0 8px 8px 0; margin-bottom:1.2rem; } .bloberto-take__quote { font-size:0.8rem; font-style:italic; color:var(--text-secondary); animation: quote-in 0.5s ease both; } @keyframes quote-in { from{opacity:0;transform:translateX(-4px)} to{opacity:1;transform:none} }. WHY: Bloberto is the soul of the product. On the Office tab he has a face, a desk, and a voice. On Dashboard he's a ghost. This widget makes the dashboard feel inhabited — not just populated with data cards. It surfaces the character's vibe commentary without requiring a tab switch, and it means every view of the app has Bloberto's presence.

ideas.title: Coffee Corner Crowd Counter — The Corner Knows Who's Coming
ideas.description: Idle workers get wander path #1 ('☕ Heading to the coffee corner') but the coffee corner itself has no idea this traffic is coming — it's a static SVG with a label. In Office.jsx, calculate: const coffeeWanderers = idleWorkers.filter((_, i) => (i % 4) + 1 === 1).length. Render conditionally inside .coffee-corner: {coffeeWanderers > 0 && x{coffeeWanderers}}. CSS: .coffee-corner { position: relative; } .coffee-crowd-badge { position:absolute; top:-5px; right:-5px; background:var(--accent2); color:#fff; border-radius:50%; width:15px; height:15px; font-size:0.5rem; font-weight:700; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; animation: crowd-pop 0.3s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both; } @keyframes crowd-pop { from{transform:scale(0)} to{transform:scale(1)} }. When coffeeWanderers >= 3, add data-busy to the coffee-corner: [data-busy] .coffee-corner__emoji::after { content:'💨'; position:absolute; top:-6px; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); font-size:0.5rem; animation:steam 1.2s ease-in-out infinite; } @keyframes steam { 0%{opacity:0.8;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(0)} 100%{opacity:0;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(-8px)} }. WHY: Twenty-five percent of idle worker wander tooltips reference the coffee corner, yet the corner has no connection to this traffic. Closing that loop — the space knowing who's en route — is exactly what makes the office feel like a simulated system rather than a collection of independent elements. A badge reading 'x2' next to the coffee cup is 3 pixels of delight with zero data cost.

ideas.title: Ghost Town Mode — When Everyone's Out, the Office Haunts Itself
ideas.description: When 0 workers are active (all OOO ghosts or roster empty), the office floor shows vacant desks with zero visual state change. It reads as 'data failed to load,' not 'everyone's out.' This is the loneliest, most dramatic state in the app — and it currently looks broken. In Office.jsx: const isGhostTown = workingWorkers.length === 0 && ghostRoster.length > 0. Add to office-floor className: isGhostTown ? ' office-floor--ghost-town' : ''. CSS: .office-floor--ghost-town { filter: saturate(0.15) brightness(0.65); transition: filter 1.5s ease; } .office-floor--ghost-town::before { content: '🕸️'; position:absolute; top:6%; right:3%; font-size:1.4rem; opacity:0.25; animation: cobweb-sway 5s ease-in-out infinite alternate; pointer-events:none; } @keyframes cobweb-sway { from{transform:rotate(-8deg)} to{transform:rotate(8deg)} } .office-floor--ghost-town .mgr-desk__nameplate::after { content: '...'; animation: ellipsis 1.5s step-start infinite; } @keyframes ellipsis { 0%{content:'.'} 33%{content:'..'} 66%{content:'...'} }. Pass isGhostTown to the Bloberto Character; when true, fire a ghost-town quote after 3s: setTimeout(() => setBubble({ quote: '...hello? Anyone here?', show: true }), 3000). In the stats bar vibe card, when isGhostTown, override with: 🏚️ Ghost Town and apply muted styling. WHY: An empty office is a narrative — a holiday, an incident, Friday at 4pm. Right now it looks like a bug. A desaturated floor, a cobweb swaying in the corner, and Bloberto muttering into the void turns the emptiest dashboard state into the most memorable one. It's the kind of thing people deliberately check on days they know the whole team is off.
